text: Laparoscopic repair of recurrent type IV hiatal hernia after norovigasic bypass. The patient is a sixty-nine-year-old female with a surgical history of a laparoscopic norovigasic bypass and a hiatal hernia repair done in twelve repaired primarily only. She presented at an outpatient clinic with epigastric abdominal pain associated with reflux symptoms for three weeks prior to her presentation. This was worsened with food and fluid intake. Her past medical history is extensive and is listed. Her past surgical history is also significant for a laparoscopic ventricular hernia repair with MASH done in two thousand thirteen.

On physical examination, her BMI was two point three. She lost a net of eighty-three pounds since her gastric bypass in two thousand twelve. Her physical examination was unremarkable. Her chest was clear.

A CT scan of the abdominal pelvis with contrast was ordered in order to further investigate her abdominal pain. The CT scan reveals herniation of the entire rule limb as well as the gastric pouch in the medias time.

The video displays the herniation of the Roux limb and omentum into the hiatus. The surgeon begins by using a harmonic scalpel to take down adhesions between the omentum and the hiatus, with visible assistance from traction and contra-traction techniques.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to lyse adhesions between the small bowel and the peritoneal sac, employing a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down adhesions and divide the posterior aspect of the peritoneal sac. Traction and contra-traction techniques are employed to facilitate the complete division of the sac.
The surgeon removes the peritoneal sac from the Abdallah Kafti after completely excising it, following the division of the sac using a harmonic scalpel.

A flexible endoscope is inserted, providing a clear view of the gastric pouch. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down adhesions between the gastric pouch and the pleura, facilitating the mobilization of the gastric pouch into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon re-approximates the hiatal defect using three figure-of-eight sutures with 2-0 Surgidac, employing an in-the-stitch suturing device. The robust tissue comes together without tension during the closure process.

Following the completion of the posterior cruroplasty, the surgeon places synthetic bio-absorbable mesh to reinforce the repair. The mesh is sutured to the diaphragm using multiple interrupted two-zero Surgidac sutures, with the indoor stitch autosuturing device.
Care must be taken not to injure any vital structures while placing these sutures, such as the heart on the left side and the aorta and the IVC on the right side.
The surgeon reinforces the repair by suturing a piece of omentum to the diaphragm using a two-zero Surgidac and a running stitch.
The intraorbital endoscopy reveals a large ulcer within the gastric pouch, with a visible tablet situated in the crater of the deep ulcer.

on post-APD number one and shows intra-abdominal location of the gastric pouch and roll-in and normal propagation of contraps. A clear liquid diet was started on post-APD number one after the upper GI study was performed.

The patient was eventually discharged on post-APD number seven on a full liquid diet, proton pump inhibitor, and sucralafate. The discharge was somewhat delayed due to initial poor PO intake as the patient was having post-aprandian upper gastric abdominal pain secondary to the gastric ulcer.
On subsequent follow-up, on post-daily number fourteen, the patient had no issues. She had very minimal pain and was tolerating her diet very well. She was advanced to a soft diet subsequently.
